Now, interesting, adding muscle and getting stronger are two totally separate strategies. Okay. Training wise. Which one means more to you? I like the numbers to be honest. I'm kind of driven by the strength numbers. But I mean, I got it, you know, I'd be lying if I was in a myth that I just would like to look better too. They go hand in hand, but there are different extremes like how much you want one or the other. Right. Bodybuilding style training is typically a very high repetition, a lot of volume, a lot of blood flow, very long workouts, powerlifting are typically shorter workouts, very, very, very intense, but totally different. So if you can imagine sets of 20s and then working up to one rep max singles, pretty much not going beyond five reps on your main reps. So that's the way it affects your body, your bones, your joints, everything else. There's a huge difference. The style or powerlifting that I found most successful has a blend of it because you still want the benefits of the high repetition, but you use those as accessories to your main reps. Okay. Strength. You know what your numbers are right now? No, my left leg number is... What's your best bench squat, remember? Squat, actually. I don't know. I was just working my leg back up. I was saying 230, I'm going to one rep max and like that. That works? Yeah. Three and a half plates per side. 365? Yeah. And to me those represent like beginner, beginner level numbers. Sure, yeah. A lot of times people will clap so at that because the technique is not tightened up. I remember I hit it, this is before I had this facility. I was just beginning my fitness journey. My numbers were around the same. I had plateaued and I hired some really great coaches. And for example, my deadlift was now 400 pounds. That went up to 600 pounds. But at the same time my body weight didn't really change all that much. My squat went from low 300s to over 500. And bench went from like 275 to 365. Nice. So I'm not a big guy either. So it's really like it's amazing what proper training and form will do and be inconsistent. I like to kind of dig into what's your motivation. Why do you want to do that? What would that mean to you? For me, it's just like when I did it before on my own, it was just I know that I felt better. I was more confident. I just had more energy. I had more fun. This was general overall, like a bigger positive. I just felt better. I know that. Now, when I try to dig for motivation, I try to dig a lot deeper. Like for me personally, my wife's pregnant with twins right now. So for me to have that energy to keep up with them, I've actually kind of switched my training around a little bit to move away from one rep maxes to more increasing my stamina and working more and being able to take care of it. So my training reflects basically my purpose of life. To take care of my family and to provide for them. So when I break it down like that, you might have something real personal that you don't have to share that with me. But I want you to think of that. Like when you step into the gym, that type of purpose is going to really increase the intensity of your workouts. It's really going to help you focus and give you that drive. That's what separates those outliers. Like if you ever heard Swartz and Eger talk about his training, his visualization techniques that he uses, like he literally thinks that he's going to die. If he doesn't do this particular thing. If you can trigger that type of mentality, you can do amazing things. And then you realize you came full of a lot more.
